81.reason: [The character constant pattern '(\\\\'|\\\\0|[^'\\\\])' is incomplete—it doesn't handle common escape sequences like \\n, \\t, etc., as used later in the matching logic, causing valid char constants to be misclassified or skipped. Also, the regex includes standalone '&' and '|' inside the [...] which causes them to be matched as tokens even when they appear alone, but the error detection only triggers if they are captured as full tokens—however, due to regex ordering, "&&" and "||" are matched first, so standalone "&" or "|" may still be matched correctly. The real issue is that the comment removal is flawed, which can cause lexical errors to be missed or misreported.]

72.reason: [This error check is placed too late: if a single '&' or '|' is matched by the [...] part of the regex, it will not be recognized as a keyword (since it's not in the keywords map), not match any other category (not digit, string, char, or identifier), and thus fall through without being caught here—because the condition `else if (token.equals("&") || token.equals("|"))` only runs if all prior conditions fail, but the token might have already been incorrectly accepted as a valid symbol if the regex allowed it and no proper validation occurred. Moreover, the regex includes '&' and '|' in the symbol set, so they are extracted as tokens, but since they are not in the keywords map, and not covered by other conditions, they reach this branch—but only if no other condition matched. However, the real issue is that the regex should NOT match standalone '&' or '|' as valid tokens at all; instead, their presence should be flagged immediately. The current design allows them to be tokenized and then optionally flagged, but due to regex structure, they may be consumed in a way that bypasses error detection.]
